doc.title: The five account types
doc.event: Sort these accounts into their families and name each one's normal balance: Cash, Accounts Payable, Service Revenue, Rent Expense, Owner's Capital, Supplies, Unearned Revenue, Accounts Receivable.

computationPaths.narration: Every account belongs to one of five families — Assets, Liabilities, Equity, Revenues, Expenses — and each family has a NORMAL BALANCE side. Debit team: Assets and Expenses (and Draws). Credit team: Liabilities, Equity, Revenues. An account grows on its normal side and shrinks on the other. That one fact runs every entry you'll ever make.

steps.label: Assets (normal: DEBIT)
steps.formulaText: Cash · Supplies · Accounts Receivable — things the business has or is owed

steps.label: Liabilities (normal: CREDIT)
steps.formulaText: Accounts Payable · Unearned Revenue — what the business owes (money or work)

steps.label: Revenues (normal: CREDIT)
steps.formulaText: Service Revenue — earnings grow equity, so they live on equity's side

steps.label: Expenses (normal: DEBIT)
steps.formulaText: Rent Expense — costs shrink equity, so they live opposite it

memorize.body: DEALER: Debits grow Draws, Expenses, Assets · credits grow Liabilities, Equity, Revenues. Say it until it's boring — then it's yours.
Unearned Revenue is a LIABILITY, not revenue — cash received for work not yet done means the business OWES the work. The name fools someone on every exam.
Why expenses are debit-normal: they REDUCE equity, and reductions live on the opposite side of the thing they reduce. Revenues grow equity, so they share equity's credit side.

questions.prompt: A client pays $500 in advance for next month's work. Unearned Revenue is a(n)…

distractors.feedback: Not revenue yet — nothing has been earned. Cash-in-advance creates a LIABILITY: the business owes $500 of work. It becomes revenue only as the work is done.
